title: Why Raw Queries Retrieve Poorly
concept: Short, ambiguous, or jargon-mismatched user queries that don't lexically or semantically match the source documents.

exercises.prompt: Why do we retrieve documents for an LLM instead of just asking it directly?

options.text: To make the LLM run faster on mobile devices.
To ground the LLM's answers in accurate, external source documents.
To train a new model from scratch.

exercises.explain: When building AI applications, we connect LLMs to external data sources (like databases) to provide accurate, up-to-date context, which is the core of RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation).

cards.title: The Search Struggle
cards.body: When users search for information, they rarely type like a textbook. They use short, messy terms, which makes it incredibly hard for databases to find the right documents.

cards.title: The Vocabulary Gap
cards.body: Raw user queries often fail because they are short, ambiguous, or use informal jargon that doesn't match the formal language saved in your database documents.

cards.title: The Library Whisperer
cards.body: Imagine trying to find a recipe book in a library by shouting 'yummy!' to the librarian. The librarian looks for books with 'yummy' in the title, missing all the gourmet cookbooks labeled 'culinary arts'.

cards.title: Real-world Search Mismatch
cards.body: If a user searches for 'Mac lagging', a basic search might miss a document titled 'Optimizing macOS CPU Throttling' because the words 'lagging' and 'throttling' don't match.

cards.title: The Vector Magic Myth
cards.body: Many believe modern vector search solves all search problems automatically. In reality, short or highly specific jargon queries can still confuse vector models, leading to irrelevant search results.

cards.title: Query vs Document
cards.body: Compare what a user actually types versus what the database is actually looking for in the documents.

exercises.prompt: Why do raw queries from users retrieve poor results from a database?

options.text: They are always written in a foreign language.
They are often short, ambiguous, or use different jargon than the documents.
They are too long for databases to process.
They bypass the embedding model entirely.

exercises.explain: Raw queries are often brief and use colloquial language, whereas database documents tend to use formal, technical language, creating a mismatch.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A search can fail even if the answer exists, simply because the user and the document author used different words for the same concept.
exercises.explain: Raw queries often fail to match documents because users use different words (synonyms, slang, or simplified terms) than the authors of the source documents.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term that describes the difference between user search terms and document text.
exercises.sentence: We call the mismatch between user search terms and database document language the vocabulary gap.
exercises.explain: The vocabulary gap refers to the difference between how users talk (informal queries) and how documents are written (formal text).

exercises.prompt: Which of these query examples represents a typical raw, conversational user query?

options.text: 'Retrieve technical documentation for CPU overheating symptoms.'
'Why is my laptop freezing up when I open many tabs?'

exercises.explain: This query uses natural, conversational, and slightly vague language ('my laptop is freezing') typical of a human user, rather than database-ready keywords.

exercises.prompt: A user searches a smartphone repair database for 'broken screen' but gets zero results, even though there are hundreds of documents about 'display fractures'. Why did this raw query fail to retrieve the right documents?

options.text: The database is offline or experiencing server downtime.
The user is deliberately using fake words to trick the search engine.
The vocabulary used by the user does not match the formal terminology in the documents.
Vector databases only work if queries are longer than 50 words.

exercises.explain: A search index relies on shared vocabulary. If a user queries 'broken screen' but the manual says 'display fracture detection', the raw terms do not overlap, leading to a complete retrieval failure. Query expansion bridges this gap.
